elements.sent-2411-001: This paper is the first in a series studying the quantum mechanical three-body problem within a modern microlocal framework.
elements.sent-2411-002: The project aims to demonstrate how the Helmholtz operator is a Fredholm map between suitable anisotropic Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-003: Notably, we will consider decoupled indices that measure microlocal decay at spatial infinity.
elements.sent-2411-004: Despite the problem's rich history, new phenomena arise under this approach, particularly regarding diffraction.
elements.sent-2411-005: The treatment of this involves the second microlocalization framework introduced by Vasy for the two-body Helmholtz operator, which does not directly extend to the three-body case.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper will clarify this structure.
elements.sent-2411-007: We will construct a pseudodifferential algebra, referred to as the three-cone algebra, which serves as a ``converse perspective'' to the second microlocalization.
elements.sent-2411-008: This algebra exhibits a scattering structure at one boundary face and a specific fibered structure (corresponding to the fibered structure for the three-body algebra) at another, which are connected by a fibered cone.
elements.sent-2411-009: We show that introducing suitable blow-ups at fiber infinities allows us to alter the three-cone algebra only at the symbolic level, thereby constructing the desired second microlocalized algebra.
elements.sent-2411-010: The presence of variable orders will also be considered.
elements.sent-2411-011: Finally, we prove basic properties for the second microlocalized algebra.
elements.sent-2411-012: On the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R ^{n}$, let us consider a collection of linear subspaces \begin{equation}\{ X_{\tindex}^{\circ} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n} \hspace*{0.5mm} ; \hspace*{0.5mm} \alpha \in \ind \}\end{equation} where we write \begin{equation}\text{dim}( X_{\tindex}^{\circ} ) = n_{\alpha}, \ \alpha \in \ind\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}\label{three-body condition on the subspaces} X_{\tindex}^{\circ} \cap X_{\tindex'}^{\circ} = \{ 0 \}, \ \tindex \neq \tindex'.\end{equation}.}

elements.section-2411-005: Basic properties of the second microlocalized algebra
elements.sent-2411-718: We now move on to discuss the central tools used to microlocally capture operator decays at infinities.
elements.sent-2411-719: For the second microlocalized operators $\Psi^{ \mathsf{m , \mathsf{r , \mathsf{l , \mathsf{v , \mathsf{b , \mathsf{s _{\mathrm{d3sc,b,res ( \Xd )$, such tools will come in two flavors: the principal symbol, which is really very pedestrian, as well as the indicial operators, which are more global in nature, and therefore warrants a more detailed discussion.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-720: In the most basic case, the principal symbol map only measures regularity, or from the phase space perspective: decay at fiber infinity.
elements.sent-2411-721: However, it's also well-known that the principal symbol can measure decays at other boundary faces of the phase space, provided that certain nice properties are satisfied (see \cite[Section 2]{PeterLinearWave1 for a brief discussion on this).}
elements.sent-2411-722: For this, one needs to make the observation that the defining estimates for $S^{\mathsf{m , \mathsf{r , \mathsf{l , \mathsf{v , \mathsf{b , \mathsf{s _{\delta ( \overline{ ^{\mathrm{d3sc,b,res T^{\ast \Xd )$ are enough to extend the principal symbol map to these faces as well, since the number of derivatives which occur in the usual procedures (i.e., symbol reductions, asymptotic summations, etc.) are enough to warrant this.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-723: Thus, $\tscbpc$ descends to an isomorphism \begin{equation}\big( { \Psi^{ m , r, l, v, b, s }_{d3sc,b,res, \delta} } \big/ { \Psi^{ m - 1 + 2 \delta, r - 1 + 2\delta, l, v - 1 + 2 \delta, b, s - 1 + 2 \delta }_{d3sc,b,res,\delta} } \big) ( \Xd ) \qquad \cong_{ \tscbpc } \big( { S^{ m , r, l, v, b , s }_{\delta} } \big/ { S^{ m - 1 + 2 \delta, r - 1 + 2\delta, l, v - 1 + 2 \delta, b , s - 1 + 2 \delta }_{\delta} } \big) ( \overline{ ^{d3sc,b,res}T^{\ast}} \Xd ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-316: \begin{equation}\big( { \Psi^{ m , r, l, v, b, s }_{d3sc,b,res, \delta} } \big/ { \Psi^{ m - 1 + 2 \delta, r - 1 + 2\delta, l, v - 1 + 2 \delta, b, s - 1 + 2 \delta }_{d3sc,b,res,\delta} } \big) ( \Xd ) \qquad \cong_{ \tscbpc } \big( { S^{ m , r, l, v, b , s }_{\delta} } \big/ { S^{ m - 1 + 2 \delta, r - 1 + 2\delta, l, v - 1 + 2 \delta, b , s - 1 + 2 \delta }_{\delta} } \big) ( \overline{ ^{d3sc,b,res}T^{\ast}} \Xd ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-724: Obviously, the calculation used in proving the above proposition, combined with the symbol estimates (\ref{symbolc estimates at cf away from dmf for first second microlocal )-(\ref{d3sc,b symbol estimate 3 ), will also be enough to show that (\ref{main theorem symbol result 1 ) and (\ref{main theorem symbol result 2 ) must hold as well.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-725: Next, we shall discuss indicial operators.
elements.sent-2411-726: In the second microlocalized setting, there are two types of indicial operators, which are separately used to measure decays at $\dff$ and $\zf$, where the principal symbol fails to do so.
elements.sent-2411-727: Instead, we will need to assume that $A$ be classical at $\dff$ and $\zf$ in some sense.
elements.sent-2411-728: In fact, to make our consideration more flexible, in the discussion below we will often make relatively weak assumption on classicality.
elements.sent-2411-729: Thus, we will for now assume that $A \in \Psi^{m, r, l,b _{\mathrm{3coc ( \Xd )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-730: Following the framework touched upon in \cite[Section 2]{AndrasBook , for any $\delta > 0$, we will say that $A$ is classical at $\dff$ modulo $\Psi_{\mathrm{3coc ^{m , r, l - \delta ,b (\Xd)$ resp.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-731: The former two faces are of course captured entirely by the principal symbol, thus classicality there can be described purely at the symbolic level.
elements.sent-2411-732: On the other hand, classicality at the global faces $\dff, \cf$ requires global asymptotic, with the standard convention requiring us to say that $A \in \Psi^{m,r,l,b _{\mathrm{3coc ( \Xd )$ is classical at $\dff$ resp.}}}
elements.sent-2411-733: Such a statement is of course the same as saying that, if we write $\hat{A _{\mathrm{3sc,b }$ resp.}}
elements.sent-2411-734: Thus we will instead resorted to the simplification (\ref{three-cone partial classicality modulo decay ), which is equivalently to, for instance, the setting where only the zeroth order terms in (\ref{asymptotic for the 3co partial quantizations in my head ) are relevant.}}
